A decision protocol Pawn, Serf,
Sheep.

Three counts. Hidden fists. Reveal.

A Note on Necessity

Rock, Paper, Scissors has served humanity admirably. For centuries it has resolved disputes, broken deadlocks, and settled arguments between individuals who could not otherwise agree on anything. It remains a masterpiece of democratic simplicity.

However. Times change.

We find ourselves at a remarkable juncture in human civilisation. The trillionaire has arrived. The replaceables have been catalogued. The distance between those who make decisions and those who must survive them has never been greater, more celebrated, or more efficiently managed.

In this context, Rock, Paper, Scissors begins to show its age. It implies equity between players. It suggests that any outcome is possible. It offers the dangerous illusion that the person across from you is your peer.

Pawn, Serf, Sheep makes no such promises.

This is a decision-making tool engineered for the low amongst the low. A game for those who understand — either by elite education or bitter daily experience — that the relevant question is never whether you will be managed, but by whom, for how long, and at what cost to your remaining dignity.

The Overlords are not your enemies. They are simply your terrain. Do not attempt to negotiate with the horizon. They will appear. They always do.

The Protocol

The mechanics are comforting in their familiarity. Three counts. Hidden fists. Reveal. Only the gestures have been updated to reflect current market realities.

The Pawn

A closed fist with the thumb placed on top like a hat. Small, blunt, pushed forward. The soldier who does not choose his battles but advances anyway.

The Pawn gesture is not just a fist — it is a fist that believes it is a choice.

The Serf

An open hand facing down, fingers together, pressing slightly into the earth. Bound to the ground. Going nowhere.

The Serf gesture is not just a lowered hand — it is a hand holding up the world that holds it down.

The Sheep

Two fingers extended from the fist like ears or horns, held to the side of the head. Instantly readable. Slightly absurd. Entirely accurate.

The Sheep gesture is not just two raised fingers — it is an apology offered before anything has happened.

Pawn — a low muttered hup.

Serf — a low, compliant exhale.

Sheep — a quiet, non-threatening baa.

System Architecture

Pawn takes Serf.The soldier enforces the state's will on the laborer.
Serf takes Sheep.The laborer shears and consumes the livestock.
Sheep takes Pawn.The flock's needs displace the soldier's utility.
Nobody takes the Overlord.The Overlord is not a variable. The Overlord is the climate. You do not defeat the climate. You endure it until it changes, or until you do.

The powerful do not play this game. They do not need to. You are playing it because you are the kind of person who reads instructions, follows rules, and hopes against all available evidence that this time, solidarity might be enough.

It probably will not be.

But a minor administrative decision — such as who will acknowledge the email thread, or who will be the last to turn off the lights — still needs to be finalized.

Play on.

Pawn, Serf, Sheep — Not a game. A reminder.

Add to Your Home Screen

Save Pawn, Serf, Sheep as an icon on your phone. It will open directly in your browser like a native app.

On iPhone

1 Open this page in Safari. If you are reading this inside the Claude app, tap the file link, then look for Open in Safari or Open in Browser in the options.
2 In Safari, tap the Share button at the bottom of the screen. It looks like a box with an arrow pointing upward.
3 Scroll down in the share menu and tap Add to Home Screen.
4 Tap Add in the top right corner. The app icon will appear on your home screen.
5 Open it from your home screen. Tap ▶ Hear the gestures to confirm sound is working.

On Android

1 Open this page in Chrome.
2 Tap the three dots menu in the top right corner.
3 Tap Add to Home Screen and confirm.

Once saved to your home screen the app opens directly in your browser. No app store required. No installation. Just the game.

A brief assessment

What Are
You Really?

Eight questions. One uncomfortable answer. No appeal process exists.

Note: This survey is satire in a post-truth world. Results are simultaneously meaningless and the most accurate thing you will read today. Participation implies awareness of the distinction. The distinction is becoming harder to locate.
1 of 8
